Step 1 — Data Pipeline
Data Quality & Cleaning
1,000,000 raw records processed → 947,752 clean records. Missing values imputed, outliers capped via IQR Winsorization.
📂
1M
Raw Records
✅
947,752
Clean Records
🔧
4 cols
Had Missing Values
⚠️
24,417
Outliers Capped
Missing Value Treatment
Before cleaning — 4 columns affected
All missing values treated: median imputation for numeric, mode imputation for categorical. Zero NaN after cleaning.
Outlier Summary (IQR Method)
Winsorization capped extreme values
Only Order Value (1.38%) and Delivery Time (1.19%) had IQR outliers. Distance, Items, Ratings were clean.
Post-Winsorization Outlier Distribution
Boxplots of all key numeric features after capping
All 6 features show clean, bounded distributions post-Winsorization. No extreme values remain. Dataset is ML-ready.
Step 2 — Exploratory Data Analysis
EDA — Patterns & Distributions
Deep dive into order patterns, delivery behaviour, ratings, and platform comparisons across 12 cities.
Delivery Distance Analysis
Distribution histogram + cumulative distribution function
Uniform distribution from 0.5–15 km confirms unbiased dataset. 10% orders within 2 km — customers order delivery instead of walking. 30.7% within 5 km — potential dark store optimisation zone.

Customer & Delivery Partner Rating Analysis
Distribution of ratings across all 947,752 orders
Customer ratings are evenly spread (mean 3.04). Delivery partner ratings skew to 4 (mean 3.80). The 0.76-point gap shows delivery is performing better than overall satisfaction — product quality / assortment is the satisfaction driver, not logistics.

Product Category Analysis
Orders, avg value and distance per category
All 7 categories are nearly equally distributed (~135–136K orders each). Dairy leads in order count (136,320). Average order values are consistent across categories (₹566–570), suggesting category doesn't drive spend — customer segment does.

Customer Age Group Behaviour
Order volume, avg value and discount rate by age
Age 45–60 drives the most orders (314,724). All age groups have identical discount rates (40%). The 25–35 group shows the highest avg order value, making them the premium targeting sweet spot.
Step 3 — Feature Engineering
8 Behavioural Score Features
Custom-built domain features encoding real business logic into ML-ready signals. Each score captures a distinct customer behaviour dimension.
Feature Mean Scores
Average value of each engineered feature across 947,752 customers
Engineered Features Correlation Matrix
How features relate to each other
Lazy Local ↔ Long Distance: −1.00 (perfect opposites). Discount Dependency near-zero correlation with all others — completely independent behaviour signal.
🎯 Convenience Score
Mean: 0.487
Low distance + low delivery time + high order value. Core quick commerce preference signal.
⚡ Delivery Efficiency
Mean: 0.950
Speed per km. Inverted delivery time/distance ratio. Highest mean of all 8 features — logistics generally efficient.
👑 Premium Buyer Score
Mean: 0.436
High order value + fast delivery preference. Identifies willing-to-pay-more customers.
🏷️ Discount Dependency
Mean: 0.503
Discount usage + low order value pattern. Highest risk feature — identifies margin-draining customers.
🛋️ Lazy Local Score
Mean: 0.522
Very low distance ordering. The "could have walked" signal. Core proof of quick commerce thesis.
🗺️ Long Distance Score
Mean: 0.474
Far-store dependency + long delivery times. Signals infrastructure gaps — no nearby stores available.
🛒 Bulk Convenience
Mean: 0.477
High item count + nearby delivery. Weekly grocery-style orders. High revenue per delivery trip.
😊 Satisfaction Index
Mean: 0.546
Customer rating + delivery partner rating composite. Near-zero correlation with distance — satisfaction is platform-independent.
Step 4 — Machine Learning
K-Means Clustering Model
Three clustering algorithms compared. K-Means K=7 selected for best business interpretability and statistical validity.
K = 7
Final Clusters
Selected over K=2 (best sil.) for business granularity
0.1781
Silhouette Score
K-Means vs Hierarchical: 0.1489
1.5578
Davies-Bouldin Score
Lower is better
3,376
Calinski-Harabasz
Higher is better
K-Means Model Selection — 4 Evaluation Metrics
Elbow, Silhouette, Davies-Bouldin and Calinski-Harabasz across K=2 to K=12
Silhouette peaks at K=2 (0.277) but that's too coarse for business strategy. K=7 selected: inertia elbow visible, DB stable at 1.558, business segments clear. K-Means outperforms DBSCAN (which found 128 micro-clusters with 30.4% noise).
Hierarchical Clustering Dendrogram
Ward Method on 500-record sample
Major split at distance ~45 confirms 2 natural macro-groups (proximity-based vs distance-based buyers). Sub-splits at 20–25 support 7-cluster granularity.
Algorithm Comparison
K-Means vs DBSCAN vs Hierarchical
Algorithm Clusters Silhouette Verdict
K-Means K=7 7 0.1781 ✓ Selected
K-Means K=2 2 0.2768 Too coarse
Hierarchical (Ward) 7 0.1489 Lower score
DBSCAN (ε=0.5) 128 −0.356 Rejected
DBSCAN's 30.4% noise points and negative silhouette confirms this dataset has continuous, overlapping distributions — not density-separated clusters. K-Means is the right choice.

7 Customer Segments Identified

The clearest proof of the quick commerce thesis. Average distance of 1.02 km — these customers could walk to the store in 12 minutes but choose delivery. Delivery is a behavioural preference, not a distance necessity.
💡 Habit-formation campaigns: streak badges, gamified ordering rewards

Step 7 — Business Intelligence
Insights & Recommendations
Executive-level findings derived from all 9 steps of the analytics pipeline. Actionable strategies for each segment and platform.
Distance vs Convenience Score — Order Density Heatmap
Where the mass market sits: distance band (Y) vs convenience score (X)
Hottest cell: 0.5–3.4 km + 0.6–0.8 convenience = 128,997 orders (classic Lazy Local zone). Second hottest: 6.3–9.2 km + 0.4–0.6 = 123,429 orders (the mass market backbone). High convenience (0.8–1.0) exists only under 6 km.
01
Why Customers Order from Nearby Stores
10% of all orders come from within 2 km — walking distance. Lazy Local Buyer 3 (avg 1.02 km, 44,288 customers) proves delivery is a behavioural utility, not a distance necessity. Drivers: time optimisation, weather avoidance, hands-free multitasking, and habit formation via app gamification.
02
Most Profitable Segment
Lazy Local Buyer 1 generates ₹12.22 Cr (22.7% of total revenue) from just 15.2% of customers. ₹846 average order value, 44% discount rate (controlled), 10-minute delivery expectation. Combined with Satisfied Loyal Customer — top 2 segments = 42.3% revenue from 30.5% of customers.
03
Best Performing Company
Swiggy Instamart leads revenue (₹7.56 Cr, ₹638 AOV) with the most balanced segment mix. Zepto's 9.64-minute delivery is the single biggest competitive moat — it attracts the highest Premium Buyer share (46%). Blinkit has the highest satisfaction rating (3.58) — best product-market fit.
04
Discount Strategy — Margin Protection
28.7% of customers (Discount Hunters) are 100% discount-dependent — the single largest margin risk. Strategy: 3-phase reduction (30%→20%→10% coupon depth over 90 days), replacing rupee discounts with product bundles and combo offers. Never run sitewide discounts — they subsidise already-loyal segments unnecessarily.
05
Delivery Zone Optimisation
Long-Distance Dependents (170,911 customers, 18%) signal dark store coverage gaps. Placing a kirana partner or micro-dark-store within 3 km of their location clusters is estimated to increase their AOV from ₹367 → ₹514 (+40%). Jio Mart's territory — Haridwar, Jaipur — shows the weakest coverage (27 min delivery, ₹434 AOV).
06
Quick Commerce Growth Opportunities
Express+ subscription: ₹99/month for guaranteed 10-min delivery — targets Lazy Local Buyer 1. Kirana integration: 3× coverage expansion without dark store CAPEX — targets Long-Distance Dependents. Digital receipt system: Standardised post-purchase records → AI spend insights → reduced return friction. Tier-2 expansion: Haridwar + Jaipur are underserved markets with no current quick commerce dominance.
Action Priority Matrix
Recommended actions sorted by business impact and timeline
Priority Segment Target Action Timeline Expected Impact
P1 — Now Lazy Local Buyer 1 Express+ subscription ₹99/mo, 10-min SLA 0–30 days +15% retention, +₹2.1 Cr ARR
P1 — Now Satisfied Loyal Customer VIP tier: zero delivery fee 6 months + early access 0–30 days −25% churn
P2 — 90 days Discount Hunter 1 & 2 3-phase discount reduction, push bundles 30–90 days +8% margin
P2 — 90 days Long-Distance Dependent Kirana partner within 3 km of cluster zones 60–90 days +40% AOV
P3 — 180 days Lazy Local Buyer 2 & 3 Streak reward gamification (5 orders = free delivery) 90–180 days +30% frequency
P3 — 180 days Jio Mart / Haridwar Dark store placement + speed infrastructure 120–180 days New market capture
